text: This is a robotic repair of a recurrent paraesophageal hernia with a toupee fundoplication. The patient is a seventy-two year old female with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and gastroesophageal reflux who is status post laparoscopic repair of a paraesophageal hernia with Nissen fundoplication approximately fourteen months prior. She initially did well after surgery, however, then developed persistent dysphagia and recurrent reflux symptoms. A barium swallow showed significant stasis of oral contrast in the barium tablet held up at the GE junction, as indicated with an arrow. An upper endoscopy revealed a loose wrap with a pouch below the GE junction with retained food in the stomach. These findings combined with the patient's symptoms were suggestive of a slipped nissen.

The patient is positioned supine with arms outstretched, and an epigastric Nathanson retractor lifts the left lobe of the liver to expose the gastroesophageal junction and the angle of His. A small type II paraesophageal hernia and a replaced left hepatic artery are visualized. The slipped Nissen fundoplication is observed distally over the stomach with unattached sutures. The hernia sac is located above the wrap anteriorly. After docking the robot, adhesions to the wrap are carefully dissected using a forced bipolar device and monopolar curved scissors. The previously placed fundoplication sutures are dissected and divided. The hernia sac is freed from the diaphragm and anterior wrap, then divided. The esophagus is circumferentially mobilized using a Penrose drain for gentle traction around the gastroesophageal junction. The anterior hiatus is noted to be dilated while posterior sutures remain intact.

The surgeon achieves adequate mobilization of the esophagus, obtaining five to six centimeters of intra-abdominal length. The posterior crura are closed with a 2-0 Ethibond suture, with four knots tied for each stitch, and an anterior suture is placed to prevent esophageal kinking. The fundus is mobilized and passed posterior to the esophagus. A 58 French bougie is inserted, and a 270-degree Toupee fundoplication is performed using three sutures. The wrap leaflets are pexied to the posterior crura with one stitch. The superior suture secures the esophageal muscle, fundus, and superior crura, while the second inferior suture connects the esophageal muscle to the fundus. The anterior and vagus nerves are identified and protected throughout the procedure.

The surgeon places the fundoplication above the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring complete hemostasis in the left upper quadrant. The bougie is carefully removed, and an upper endoscopy confirms the wrap is positioned above the Z-line. A retroflexion view shows a 270-degree wrap. After retrieving the needles and Penrose drain, all instruments are removed, and the robot is undocked, with no visible esophageal or gastric injuries.

The total operative time was one hour and four minutes. Post-operatively, the patient was monitored on telemetry overnight on the surgical floor and allowed clear liquids. On step day one, the patient was advanced to a pureed diet and discharged home that afternoon.
